title: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
summary: The repository XML is a version of the University of Michigan CME00101 electronic text, encoded from Hermann Varnhagen's 1879 transcription on printed pp. 229-244. Varnhagen transcribed British Library Royal MS 18 A X, ff. 61v-66v, and collated other witnesses in apparatus excluded from the XML. A complete Internet Archive scan of Anglia volume 2 and the exact DIMEV v1.0 dataset are cached with fixity; no complete public facsimile of the Royal manuscript was found as of 2026-07-13.
review_notes: The source chain distinguishes the repository artifact, U-M digital encoding, 1879 scholarly edition, medieval manuscript witnesses, printed-volume facsimile, and DIMEV catalogue dataset.

DIMEV v1.0 calls record 605 French/macaronic and gives a malformed Royal explicit. Those fields conflict with the direct Royal transcription and current British Library catalogue and are documented rather than propagated.
The cached Anglia PDF is a complete scan of the printed volume, not a manuscript facsimile.

entities.label: General Corpus repository artifact CME00101.xml
entities.description: The XML file in the CME submodule used as the General Corpus source artifact for CME00101.

date_statements.label: Recorded XML revision
date_statements.notes: The TEMPHEAD records a 9-20-16 source cleanup and structural review; the repository artifact is fixed here at the cited CME submodule commit.

notes: IDG, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00101.
The artifact contains the poem heading through 'Amen' and the explicit; it does not contain Varnhagen's surrounding introduction or notes.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding CME00101
entities.description: The keyed electronic text represented by the U-M stable item identifier CME00101 and preserved in the repository artifact.
entities.reproduction_format: SGML/XML transcription with structural, page, and manuscript-folio milestones
notes: The exact item page was not machine-readable during this review, but the stable U-M item identifier, the collection landing page, and the local artifact agree on CME00101.
The encoding's PB sequence 229-244 and its text correspond to Varnhagen's Royal-witness transcription.

entities.label: Varnhagen's 1879 edition of the Royal body-and-soul debate
entities.description: Hermann Varnhagen's Royal MS 18 A X transcription, with German introduction, textual notes, and comparison of other witnesses, in the first section of his Anglia article.

bibliographic.citation: Hermann Varnhagen, 'Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten: I-III,' Anglia 2 (1879): 225-255.
bibliographic.title: Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten: I-III

entities.reproduction_format: Diplomatic-critical printed transcription with editorial normalization and notes
notes: The article's first section, 'Zu den Streitgedichten zwischen Körper und Seele,' runs from p. 225 into p. 252. The Royal transcription represented by CME00101 occupies pp. 229-244.
The German introduction begins on p. 225 and continues onto p. 229; editorial notes begin immediately after the Royal explicit on p. 244. Neither surrounding portion is encoded in CME00101.

entities.label: London, British Library, Royal MS 18 A X
entities.description: A vellum codex of English theological works and poems; the body-and-soul debate occupies ff. 61v-66v as item 10.

identifiers.scheme: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record ID

date_statements.label: British Library manuscript date
date_statements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

date_statements.label: Auchinleck Project date for the Royal witness
date_statements.notes: Preserved as a separate scholarly dating rather than merged into a falsely exact date.

holding.notes: Western Manuscripts, Royal Collection, London. The catalogue records former ownership by John, Lord Lumley.

notes: The work occupies ff. 61v-66v; the next catalogued item begins on f. 67.
Stanza IV, corresponding to lines 25-32, was added by another hand on a small inserted vellum strip foliated 62.
The poem has 67 eight-line stanzas, with two verse lines written per manuscript line according to the British Library description.
The current British Library catalogue's Digitised Content field is blank. This is recorded as a dated access finding, not as proof that no digitization can ever exist.
The Royal text is Middle English with short Latin speech labels and a Latin explicit; it does not support DIMEV v1.0's work-level French/macaronic classification.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: The earliest currently identified manuscript witness of the Middle English body-and-soul debate, used as a collateral comparison witness by Varnhagen.

date_statements.label: Manuscript date
date_statements.value: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century

holding.notes: The body-and-soul debate is reported at ff. 200v-203r by the Database of Middle English Romance; DIMEV v1.0 reports ff. 200v-203v.

notes: The Database of Middle English Romance localizes this manuscript to the West Midlands.
DIMEV notes a four-line French benediction unique to this witness. It does not justify labeling the Royal text or the whole work French.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Anglia, volume 2 (1879)
entities.description: A 596-page digital scan of the complete printed Anglia volume containing Varnhagen's article and Royal transcription. It reproduces the printed edition, not Royal MS 18 A X.

date_statements.label: Internet Archive public item record
date_statements.notes: The metadata records public item processing and a 2008 copyright-status review; it is not the publication date of the volume.

entities.reproduction_format: Complete-volume PDF with OCR text and item metadata
notes: Printed page 229 is PDF page 241 and printed page 244 is PDF page 256 in the cached file.
The item was contributed by the Robarts Library, University of Toronto; Internet Archive metadata identifies the scanner as uoft.

entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, record 605
entities.description: The versioned DIMEV dataset and online record describing this work, its seven manuscript witnesses, bibliography, form, incipit, titles, and subject terms.

bibliographic.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, and project contributors, Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0 (2025), record 605, DOI 10.5281/zenodo.14585961.
bibliographic.title: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

entities.reproduction_format: Versioned research dataset distributed as a ZIP archive, with a corresponding online record
notes: The cached ZIP is the exact v1.0 release even though Zenodo now points users toward a newer version.
Record 605's French/macaronic language field and its Royal-witness explicit conflict with direct sources and are treated as catalogue-data errors, not textual evidence.

access.access_method: Open the current British Library Archives and Manuscripts record.
Consult in London with a valid British Library Reader Pass and advance request; the catalogue flags a letter of introduction as required.
notes: Access conditions can change and should be reconfirmed before travel.

access.access_method: No complete public facsimile was located through the exact BL record, legacy BL manuscript route, DIMEV witness record, or corroborating manuscript portals.
notes: The BL record's Digitised Content field is blank, and the legacy FullDisplay URL redirects to a generic digitized-manuscripts page. This is a dated negative finding, not an assertion that no private or future digitization exists.

access.access_method: Submit the exact shelfmark and requested folios to British Library Imaging Services; request either ff. 61v-66v or a complete-codex reproduction and obtain a quote and written reuse terms.
access.format: Made-to-order digital photographs or scans, subject to provider assessment
notes: For a complete physical-object facsimile request, specify covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and all folios, not only the poem leaves.
Ordering images and obtaining permission to reproduce them are separate steps.

access.access_method: Consult the Database of Middle English Romance manuscript description and DIMEV witness record; confirm against the current Bodleian catalogue for reproduction work.

rights.component: Repository XML artifact and its markup
rights.basis: The CME submodule root LICENSE grants the Unlicense to 'software', but it does not expressly state whether that grant covers the encoded textual data in CME00101.xml.
notes: The uncertainty concerns the digital artifact and markup, not the fifteenth-century wording or the 1879 edition.
Do not infer an open data license solely from a software-focused root license.

rights.component: U-M Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse digital materials
rights.basis: The U-M collection page identifies the collection content as public domain in the United States and exposes the DPLA No Copyright-United States rights statement.
notes: This is a provider assertion limited to the United States, not a worldwide license and not a license for British Library manuscript images.

rights.component: Varnhagen's 1879 text, editorial introduction, and notes
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1879, and Internet Archive metadata for the scanned volume states NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT with no visible copyright notice and the stated 1879 date.
notes: No open license is asserted; public-domain status and licensing are different claims.

rights.component: Internet Archive PDF, OCR, and item metadata for the scanned 1879 volume
rights.basis: Internet Archive records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the item, citing the 1879 date and absence of a visible notice.
notes: The item metadata contains no license URL. The cache is research storage and is not a redistribution grant.

rights.basis: The exact Zenodo v1.0 record and LICENSE in the cached release specify Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.
notes: Attribution requirements apply to reuse of DIMEV's modern dataset and documentation; this does not alter the medieval text's status.

rights.component: Medieval textual content attested on ff. 61v-66v
rights.basis: The British Library dates the witness to the first half of the fifteenth century. This assessment concerns the medieval wording only, not ownership of the object, a modern transcription, or any photograph.
notes: Physical access, photography, and image reuse remain separately controlled.

rights.component: Any digital images newly supplied by British Library Imaging Services
rights.basis: British Library imaging terms require the customer to investigate third-party rights and obtain written permission for reproduction; the terms and permissions would need to be captured for the files actually supplied.
notes: Do not infer image-level public-domain or redistribution rights from the age of the manuscript text.
Reassess this statement against the quote, order confirmation, delivered-file notice, and written permission if images are obtained.

rights.component: Medieval textual content of the body-and-soul debate
rights.basis: The witness is dated to the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century. This assessment concerns the medieval wording, not modern descriptions, transcriptions, or photographs.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Royal MS 18 A X version among the known manuscript witnesses
editorial_practices.representation: A complete 536-line base transcription under siglum R
editorial_practices.description: Varnhagen selected Royal MS 18 A X as the base text for the printed transcription and treated other manuscript versions in the accompanying comparison and notes.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Readings in Royal, Laud, Auchinleck, Vernon, and Digby witnesses
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial notes and a later comparison of the Laud text
editorial_practices.description: Varnhagen records emendations and conjectures in notes and compares the Royal text with four other witnesses known to him; this apparatus is not encoded in CME00101.
editorial_practices.scope: Section I apparatus following the Royal explicit on p. 244 and the Laud-text comparison beginning on p. 249
notes: Varnhagen reports correcting only a small number of readily reparable sense-disrupting errors and otherwise leaving errors in the text with conjectures in the notes; he says he did not emend simply to improve rhyme or assonance.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Expanded manuscript abbreviations and editorial supplies
editorial_practices.representation: Italic type and bracketed supplies in the printed transcription
editorial_practices.description: Varnhagen uses typographic distinction for supplied or expanded letters; the XML preserves many such italic spans with HI REND='italic'.
notes: The italics are editorial signals inherited from the print source, not evidence of manuscript italics.

editorial_practices.representation: Verse beginnings and proper names capitalized editorially
editorial_practices.description: Varnhagen states that he supplied capitals at verse beginnings and for proper names.

editorial_practices.representation: Editorial punctuation and normalized word division
editorial_practices.description: Varnhagen supplied punctuation, normalized word division, and distinguished u/v and i/j in the printed text.
notes: The edition also regularizes a small number of specified 'fro' forms; it is not a purely diplomatic character-for-character transcription.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Varnhagen's article with German introduction, Royal transcription, and textual apparatus
editorial_practices.representation: Only the Royal transcription, from heading through Amen and explicit
editorial_practices.description: The encoding selects printed pp. 229-244 and omits the preceding introduction and following notes, while preserving the printed page sequence and internal speech headings.
notes: The eight DIV2 divisions are dialogue or scene sections within one poem. One 16-line LG merges two source stanzas, producing 66 encoded LG elements for a 67-stanza text.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD; IDG/BIBNO/VID; TEXT; DIV1/DIV2; PB; LG/L; MILESTONE; CLOSER
evidence.summary: The fixed repository artifact identifies CME00101, records a 2016 structural cleanup, and encodes Varnhagen's Royal transcription from printed pp. 229-244.

evidence.citation: Structural and textual inspection of CME00101.xml, 2026-07-13
evidence.summary: Inspection confirmed one poem, eight nested dialogue/scene divisions, 66 line groups, 536 verse lines, 16 printed-page breaks, folio milestones from 61v through 66v, and one 16-line LG spanning encoded lines 433-448.

evidence.citation: Hermann Varnhagen, 'Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten: I-III,' Anglia 2 (1879): 225-255
evidence.locator: section I, pp. 225-252; Royal transcription, pp. 229-244; notes after the explicit on p. 244; Laud comparison beginning p. 249
evidence.summary: Direct inspection confirms the article title and publication, Royal MS 18 A X as base witness, the 67-stanza/536-line transcription, Varnhagen's editorial statement, the exact selection represented by the XML, and the collateral witness comparisons.

The item record identifies the full-volume scan, its Robarts/University of Toronto contribution and uoft scan provenance, 596-page PDF deliverable, and a United States possible-copyright-status of NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT based on the 1879 date and no visible notice.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection page
evidence.summary: The official collection page identifies the U-M corpus and marks collection content public domain in the United States with a DPLA No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Digital Collections stable item identifier CME00101
evidence.summary: The stable U-M identifier names the digital item represented by the repository artifact. Automated retrieval was denied during this review, so no unobserved item-page fields are asserted.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 18 A X, record 040-002107426
evidence.summary: The catalogue supplies the current shelfmark and record identifiers, first-half-fifteenth-century date, vellum support, extent and dimensions, Lumley provenance, and an item-level description of the 67-stanza poem including inserted f. 62, rubric, incipit, ending, and explicit. Its Digitised Content field is blank.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts request route linked from the Royal MS 18 A X catalogue record
evidence.summary: The current catalogue and linked request route establish advance-request and Reader Pass requirements; the exact record also flags a letter of introduction.

The service accepts shelfmark- and folio-specific requests, charges fees, distinguishes photography of originals from scanning surrogates, and supplies completed images electronically subject to assessment.

evidence.citation: British Library, Imaging Services terms and conditions, updated January 2026
evidence.summary: The terms make the customer responsible for rights investigation and require separate written permission for reproduction of supplied images; they therefore do not support an automatic open-reuse inference.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 605 (IMEV/NIMEV 351)
evidence.locator: work record, Royal witness entry, Laud witness entry, and bibliography
evidence.summary: The online record identifies the work, seven manuscript witnesses, stanza form, subjects, and Varnhagen bibliography. The Royal shelfmark/folios and edition pages agree with direct sources, while its French/macaronic label and Royal explicit do not.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, Zenodo record 14585961, DOI 10.5281/zenodo.14585961
evidence.summary: The exact release page identifies v1.0, its 2025-01-01 publication, creators, DOI, and CC BY 4.0 license. The cached ZIP comment fixes the release to Git commit 5ad5f592868471b44fc72f6e2f42db419780b42c; local ZIP integrity testing passed.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Auchinleck Manuscript, 'The Debate between the Body and the Soul' headnote
evidence.summary: The NLS scholarly project distinguishes manuscript versions of the same work and reports 67 stanzas and a c. 1425-1450 date for the Royal witness, alongside the earlier Laud witness.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108
evidence.summary: The manuscript description dates Laud Misc. 108 to the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century, localizes it to the West Midlands, and places the debate at ff. 200v-203r.

evidence.citation: Dated search for a public complete facsimile of British Library Royal MS 18 A X, 2026-07-13
evidence.summary: The current BL record has no Digitised Content link; the legacy BL URL redirects generically; DIMEV supplies no facsimile; and Arlima independently marks the manuscript not digitized. A constructed IIIF route did not expose a public manifest. No complete public facsimile was found, while physical consultation and made-to-order imaging remain available.
notes: This negative finding is qualified by date and search scope and must not be paraphrased as proof of nonexistence.

evidence.summary: The root license contains the Unlicense dedication but scopes its operative declaration to 'software'. It does not expressly identify the XML text corpus as licensed data.

open_questions.question: Can a complete public facsimile of Royal MS 18 A X be located or obtained?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether an unlinked digital surrogate, preservation microfilm, or forthcoming digitization exists for the complete codex.

If none is publicly available, request a quote for the complete physical object, explicitly including covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, and all folios; if cost prevents that, request at minimum ff. 61v-66v.
Record the provider's written reuse terms separately before adding or redistributing any supplied images.
notes: The lack of a public facsimile prevents direct verification of Varnhagen's readings and the inserted f. 62 against current images.

open_questions.question: Does the CME submodule's Unlicense apply to the XML textual data and markup, or only to software?
next_steps: Seek an explicit repository statement identifying the licensed components.
Until clarified, preserve the separate U-M United States public-domain assertion without treating it as an open worldwide license for this repository artifact.

open_questions.question: Does the body-and-soul debate in Laud Misc. 108 end on f. 203r or f. 203v?
next_steps: Check the current Bodleian item-level catalogue or a complete manuscript facsimile.
Retain both source reports until direct evidence resolves the discrepancy.
notes: DIMEV v1.0 reports ff. 200v-203v; the Database of Middle English Romance reports ff. 200v-203r. This does not affect the Royal-based CME00101 source chain.

open_questions.question: Can the exact U-M encoding date, encoding guidelines, and version history behind CME00101 be documented?
next_steps: Consult archived U-M project documentation or ask the collection maintainers for item-level provenance.
Compare any recovered master encoding with the fixed repository artifact before asserting direct identity.
notes: The artifact's source identity and edition selection are well supported; the detailed institutional production history remains undocumented in the sources retrieved.
